problems.prompt: I run growth for a recipe app. We have 80K monthly actives and growth has been flat for five months despite spending more on paid acquisition each month (CAC has crept from $4 to $9). The product lets users save recipes and follow other home cooks; cooks who get followers post more, and good posts pull in new signups via shared links. Right now sharing is buried behind two taps and we don't notify a cook when someone follows them. I have one quarter of eng time and a fixed budget. Where should I invest to actually restart growth, and why not just keep scaling paid?
problems.failure_mode: Defaults to optimizing the paid-acquisition parameter (raise budget, tweak CAC/creative) — a leaky-bucket additive channel with rising cost — instead of recognizing and strengthening the dormant compounding loop (post → followers → notification → more posting → more shared links → signups), which offers far higher long-run leverage; also ignores that flat growth signals a balancing constraint capping the current loop.

problems.prompt: Our 30-person engineering org keeps swinging between extremes. When incidents pile up, leadership declares a 'stability quarter,' we freeze features and over-staff reliability work; once things are quiet, they declare a 'velocity quarter,' pull everyone onto features, and reliability work stops entirely. Then incidents creep back up a couple months later and we swing again. We've done this four times in two years. Each swing feels justified in the moment. How would you break this cycle, and what's actually driving it?
problems.failure_mode: Frames each swing as an isolated prioritization judgment call (and may just recommend 'better balance') rather than seeing a delayed balancing loop: corrective action is taken based on the current incident count, but reliability investment pays off only after a multi-month lag, so the org overcorrects in both directions and oscillates — the fix is to shorten/lead the feedback (leading indicators, steady reliability allocation) and damp the swing, not to argue which mode is right.

problems.prompt: Our internal developer platform team is drowning. Every product squad files support tickets when our CI/CD or service templates break or confuse them, and we've made it a point of pride to respond fast and fix things for them directly. Ticket volume has grown ~15% month over month for a year; we've added two engineers and response times still got worse, not better. Leadership's proposed solution is to hire two more platform engineers to 'catch up.' Before we do that, tell us whether more headcount will fix this and what else to consider.
problems.failure_mode: Accepts the linear 'more demand → more capacity' framing and endorses hiring, missing that heroically fixing tickets directly is a symptomatic fix that suppresses the pressure to address fundamentals (docs, self-service, root-cause defects), letting demand keep compounding; added capacity briefly relieves the symptom and reinforces dependency, so the high-leverage move is to invest in the fundamental solution and add balancing feedback, not staff up.

problems.prompt: Our SaaS product's churn rate has been slowly climbing for eight months — from 2.1% to 3.8% monthly. To compensate, the sales team has been increasing outbound volume and discounting deals to maintain net revenue. The discounted deals come in at lower ACVs, which means we need even more volume to hit targets, and the team is stretched. Meanwhile, the churning customers all mention 'unmet expectations' in their exit surveys. Sales leadership wants to double down on outbound to offset churn. Before approving the budget, help us understand the dynamics at play.
problems.failure_mode: Accepts the sales team's framing that more outbound is the lever to pull, missing the reinforcing decline loop where unmet expectations drive churn, churn pressure drives discounting and over-promising to close deals faster, which generates even more unmet expectations — so the sales acceleration feeds the exact dynamic causing churn rather than addressing the expectation gap.

problems.prompt: Our on-call rotation has become brutal. Alerts fire every few hours, most are low-severity or noisy, and the on-call engineer spends more time dismissing alerts than investigating real issues. As a result, engineers have started muting alert channels and delaying investigation. Last month a real P1 was missed for 40 minutes because it blended into the noise. Our proposed fix is to add more detailed runbooks so engineers respond to alerts faster. Is that the right fix?
problems.failure_mode: Accepts runbooks as the lever and focuses on response speed, missing the reinforcing loop: high alert noise reduces engineer responsiveness, which causes real incidents to be missed, which erodes trust in monitoring, which leads to more muting, which increases noise tolerance — so better runbooks don't close the loop; reducing alert noise (raising signal-to-noise, deleting duplicate alerts) is the fundamental fix that changes the loop's dynamics.

problems.prompt: We run a marketplace for freelance developers. For the first 18 months growth was explosive — more clients meant more developers signed up, which attracted more clients. For the past 6 months, new client acquisition has stalled despite our developer supply growing 20%. We've been adding features and improving the client UX, but nothing moves the needle. What do you think is actually happening here?
problems.failure_mode: Diagnoses a feature or UX problem and recommends more feature work, rather than identifying that the reinforcing loop (more clients → more developer supply → more clients) has hit a limiting constraint — likely match quality, trust, or market saturation on one side — which caps the flywheel; the intervention is to identify and address the constraint, not to keep adding fuel to a loop that's already hitting its ceiling.

problems.prompt: We maintain a shared staging environment for 8 product teams. It's constantly broken: someone deploys a half-finished feature, it corrupts shared state, and other teams can't test. Teams respond by deploying directly to production to work around staging, which causes production incidents, which makes leadership demand more staging stability, which results in more restrictions on production deploys, which causes more staging workaround pressure. The ops team wants to add more staging monitors and deploy gates. Will that fix it?
problems.failure_mode: Accepts the monitors-and-gates remedy as additive hardening, missing the core escalation loop: staging unreliability → production bypasses → production incidents → tighter production gates → more staging pressure → staging unreliability; monitors reduce visibility lag but don't break the loop — the fundamental fix is team-isolated staging environments that eliminate the shared-state coupling.

problems.prompt: Our product sends automated email digests to users. Open rates used to be 28%, but over 18 months they've declined to 11%. To compensate, we've increased email frequency from weekly to daily, hoping more touchpoints will compensate for lower per-email engagement. Monthly active users have dropped 8% in the same period. Our marketing team is now proposing to add push notifications as an additional channel. Walk us through the dynamics before we add another channel.
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates the push notification channel on its own merits rather than identifying the reinforcing disengagement loop: declining engagement → increased frequency to compensate → further erosion of attention (unsubscribes, inbox filtering) → even lower engagement per send → frequency escalation again; adding push notifications extends the same dynamic to a new channel rather than reversing the loop, and the fundamental fix is restoring value per communication.

problems.prompt: Our engineering team has a culture problem: code reviews are cursory and often just rubber-stamped. When we ask why, engineers say 'there's no time — we have too many PRs in the queue.' Because reviews are weak, bugs slip through, which means firefighting, which means less time for reviews, which means the queue grows. A scrum master suggested we add a 'code review timebox' policy: PRs must be reviewed within 24 hours or auto-approved. Is that a good solution?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates the timebox policy without identifying the vicious cycle structure: firefighting reduces review time, which reduces review quality, which increases bugs and firefighting — so the timebox reduces queue pressure but doesn't address quality (auto-approvals would further degrade it), and the loop continues; the right intervention targets the loop's gain (reduce firefighting, not just review speed).

problems.prompt: I'm the CTO of a 15-person startup. For the past year, I've been the bottleneck: every technical decision above a certain threshold waits for my approval. I'm now in 30+ hours of meetings a week, and I've been telling myself that as soon as the company stabilizes I'll delegate more. But the more I approve and decide, the more decisions get routed to me, and the more critical I become to daily operations. My co-founder says 'just delegate more,' but it feels dangerous to step back. What's going on here?
problems.failure_mode: Treats this as a delegation skill or time-management problem, missing the reinforcing loop: centralizing decisions increases the CTO's criticality, which makes delegation feel higher-risk, which increases centralization further; the 'wait until stable' logic actively prevents the stability it's waiting for, and the fix is a structural change (decision frameworks, empowered deputies) that breaks the loop rather than trying harder within it.

problems.prompt: We adopted sprint velocity as our primary engineering productivity metric 18 months ago. Teams quickly learned to split stories smaller, avoid spikes, and decline to include tech debt work in sprints (no story points). Velocity has gone up 40% on paper, but our release cadence has actually slowed and customer-reported bugs have doubled. Leadership wants to add more metrics to catch the gaming. Should we add metrics, and what's the core issue?
problems.failure_mode: Recommends adding metrics without seeing the balancing loop that any measurable target will generate: engineers optimize the measured variable, which improves the metric while degrading the unmeasured reality — adding more metrics extends the gaming surface rather than fixing it; the loop's dynamics require changing the goal (outcomes, not velocity) not adding measurement pressure.

problems.prompt: A consumer social app has a content recommendation algorithm tuned to maximize daily active usage. Engagement has grown steadily for 12 months. But retention past month 3 has dropped from 65% to 38%, and our NPS has gone negative. Users who've churned say they 'felt compelled to keep scrolling but never felt satisfied.' Some long-time users are publicly deleting the app. Our growth team wants to double down on the engagement optimizations since they're working. What do they need to understand before doing so?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates 'engagement is up so keep going' at face value, missing the balancing loop operating on a longer timescale: engagement maximization drives compulsive/unsatisfying usage patterns, which depletes user wellbeing and satisfaction, which triggers eventual rejection and churn — the short-term reinforcing loop (engagement → data → better recommendations → more engagement) is being undermined by a longer balancing loop that only becomes visible in 90-day retention, and optimizing the short loop harder will accelerate the longer one.

problems.prompt: Our API gateway has been auto-scaling horizontally for six months, and our cloud bill is up 300%. We traced it to a retry storm: a downstream service has elevated error rates (~8%), clients retry failed requests up to 5 times with no backoff, and each retry hits the gateway — so real traffic of 10K RPS is generating ~50K gateway requests. We added more gateway capacity to handle the load, which absorbed the cost but didn't fix the error rate. What's actually going on and what should we fix?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates gateway scaling as the right lever since it 'handles the load,' missing the reinforcing loop: errors → retries → amplified load → slower downstream → more errors → more retries; adding gateway capacity absorbs the symptom without closing the loop, so the storm grows with any demand increase — the fix is breaking the loop (fix the downstream error rate, add backoff/jitter, limit retry amplification factor) not absorbing more of its output.

problems.prompt: We run a B2B SaaS and we've been offering increasingly generous free trials to improve top-of-funnel conversion — first 14 days, then 30, now 60 days. Conversion from trial to paid has stayed flat at about 12%, and our support costs have gone up because trial users generate nearly as many tickets as paid users. Sales is suggesting we extend trials to 90 days to give prospects more time to see value. Assess this proposal.
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates whether 90 days is better than 60 days without identifying the structural issue: extending trial length is a parameter adjustment that hasn't moved conversion (flat at 12% across all trial lengths), suggesting the loop isn't 'time to see value' but something else (onboarding, activation, job-fit) — more trial time doesn't fix a broken activation loop, and the escalation pattern of longer trials to fix flat conversion is itself a signal of misdiagnosis.

problems.prompt: We have a machine learning model that predicts which support tickets need urgent escalation. The model's outputs are reviewed by a team of 5 tier-1 agents who then escalate or close tickets. Over time, the agents have started trusting the model implicitly and rarely overriding it. When we audited last quarter, we found the model's precision on 'urgent' had dropped from 84% to 61%, but escalation rates were unchanged. What do you think is happening and how do we fix it?
problems.failure_mode: Diagnoses a model performance problem and recommends retraining, without identifying the feedback loop collapse: the model's labels were initially validated by human judgment, but as agents stopped overriding, the model's predictions became the ground truth for its own training data — a closed reinforcing loop that drifts from reality; the fix requires restoring independent human validation signals before retraining.

problems.prompt: Our open-source project had a surge of GitHub stars and contributor PRs 18 months ago after a popular blog post. Maintainers were excited and reviewed PRs quickly, which attracted more contributors, which grew the issue queue, which made review turnaround slower, which led several core contributors to stop opening PRs. Now the issue queue has 340 open items, review time is 6+ weeks, and new contributors rarely return after their first PR sits for a month. Two remaining maintainers are burnt out. The project's Slack suggests 'we just need more maintainers.' Will more maintainers fix this?
problems.failure_mode: Accepts 'more maintainers' as the obvious resource fix, missing the dynamics: the reinforcing contributor growth loop was undermined by a balancing loop with delay (queue depth → slow review → contributor departure → queue grows further) that became dominant; adding maintainers to the current structure absorbs queue temporarily but doesn't change the bottleneck dynamics or the contributor experience loop — the structural fix is triaging the queue radically and creating contributor experience feedback that attracts motivated reviewers.

problems.prompt: We're a healthcare SaaS. Our data team noticed that as we've added more dashboards and self-service reporting, the volume of ad hoc data requests to the data team has actually increased — not decreased. Teams are now using self-service reports as a starting point and immediately asking for more customization. The data team lead says 'we need to build even better self-service tools to reduce requests.' Is that the right diagnosis?
problems.failure_mode: Accepts more self-service tooling as the escalating fix, missing the reinforcing loop: better self-service tools increase data literacy and appetite, which generates more complex questions that require the data team, which increases requests — the fix isn't adding more self-service capacity but understanding what underlying business question is generating the demand and whether that question can be answered definitively.

problems.prompt: Our deployment pipeline takes 42 minutes from merge to production. Because it's slow, engineers batch multiple PRs together and merge them in groups, which makes debugging deploys harder when something breaks, which reduces confidence in deploying often, which causes engineers to accumulate even larger batches before merging. Our current proposal is to add a faster parallel test runner to cut the pipeline to 30 minutes. Will that meaningfully change developer behavior?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates the speed improvement as a direct fix without identifying the balancing loop: slow pipeline → batching behavior → larger blast radius → risk aversion → more batching; reducing pipeline time from 42 to 30 minutes doesn't change the batching behavior or incentive structure enough to break the loop — the structural fix is making small-batch deploys the path of least resistance (automated merge queues, feature flags, incremental rollouts), not just speeding the existing flow.

problems.prompt: A fintech startup I advise has been growing revenue 15% month-over-month for eight months, fueled by a referral program where existing customers earn 1% cashback for every referral who deposits $500+. The unit economics look great because CAC from referrals is a fraction of paid channels. They're planning to triple the referral bonus to accelerate the loop. What should they understand about the dynamics before doing that?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates whether tripling the bonus will proportionally triple growth without analyzing the reinforcing loop's limits: referral networks are bounded (each person has a finite network), tripling bonuses attracts fraud and gaming (synthetic referrals, round-tripping), and the loop's growth will saturate as high-propensity referrers exhaust their networks — these limiting dynamics make a simple 3x bonus → 3x growth assumption wrong, and without a balancing mechanism the bonus escalation creates its own risks.

problems.prompt: We've been running a developer community forum for our API platform. For the first year, it was active and helpful. Over the past six months it's become quieter. We noticed that fewer members are posting, which means fewer people are getting quick answers, which means people ask support directly instead of the forum, which means the forum looks even more dormant to newcomers who don't bother posting. Our community manager wants to hire a developer advocate to post more content. Is that the right response?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates the developer advocate hire on its own merits without identifying the reinforcing decay loop: fewer posts → fewer answers → direct support diversion → even fewer posts → dormancy perception → fewer new posts; a developer advocate posting content addresses the volume symptom but doesn't close the loop — the intervention that breaks it is ensuring unanswered questions get visible responses to restore the expectation of reciprocity.

problems.prompt: We're a 4-year-old startup with 60 engineers. Our sprint planning meetings have been getting longer — from 45 minutes to 3 hours over two years. As they've gotten longer, fewer engineers prepare in advance (why bother if it's going to be a long conversation anyway), which means more context is established in the meeting itself, which makes meetings longer. We added a mandatory pre-read requirement 3 months ago but it didn't help. Managers are now proposing to split planning into multiple smaller meetings. Will that work?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates splitting meetings as an additive intervention without recognizing the reinforcing loop: long meetings reduce prep motivation, which increases in-meeting context load, which extends meetings; splitting meetings into multiple sessions spreads the same low-prep dynamic across more calendar time, and the loop persists — the structural fix requires changing the incentive for preparation (not just a mandate) and reducing the in-meeting redundancy that low prep creates.

problems.prompt: Our product has a free tier and a $99/month pro tier. We've noticed that free users who are active for 30+ days rarely upgrade. We've tried in-app upgrade prompts, discount offers, and email campaigns — all with minimal effect on conversion. A product manager says we should make the free tier less capable to push users toward pro. Before we make the free tier more restrictive, help us think through what might actually be driving this pattern.
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates the free-tier restriction as a conversion lever without examining the balancing loop at play: free-tier users who haven't upgraded after 30 days have likely found the free tier sufficient for their job — restricting the free tier creates churn (users leave rather than pay) rather than conversion, and the relevant question is whether there's a reinforcing loop connecting free engagement to a natural upgrade trigger that's currently absent.

problems.prompt: Our data pipeline has been getting slower for eight months — batch jobs that used to complete in 2 hours now take 7+. The data team's response has been to increase compute cluster size each time a job approaches its SLA, which keeps jobs within SLA but doesn't address the slowdown. The cluster is now 4x its original size and costs $40K/month. The team says the next step is to move to a larger instance type. Before doing that, help us diagnose what's actually happening.
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates the instance upgrade as the natural next step without seeing the pattern: repeated buffer/parameter increases (cluster size) absorbing a growing demand signal without investigating the underlying reinforcing growth driver — the slowdown could be a data volume growth loop, a query complexity accumulation loop, or an inefficiency compounding loop, and each requires a different intervention that isn't 'add more compute.'

problems.prompt: We run a two-sided marketplace connecting freelance translators with clients. We've been adding translators aggressively — we now have 12,000 registered translators for 800 active monthly clients. Despite the supply surplus, clients still report difficulty finding quality translators quickly, and translator earnings have dropped because they're competing for fewer jobs. Our supply team wants to keep growing translator supply to ensure clients always have options. What should they understand about the current dynamics?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates 'more supply ensures availability' without identifying that the system has passed a tipping point: excess supply dilutes translator earnings, which reduces translator quality and engagement (they move to other platforms), which worsens client match quality, which reduces client trust, which reduces demand — a reinforcing quality-degradation loop; adding more low-earnings translators accelerates this loop rather than fixing it.

problems.prompt: I lead product at an edtech startup. Our core loop: students complete lessons, get badges, which encourages them to complete more lessons. Completion rates were great for the first 8 months. Now, 12 months in, daily lesson completions are declining even though new signups are still healthy. Users who've been on the platform longest are completing the fewest lessons. Engagement team wants to add more badge types and a leaderboard. Should they?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates adding badges/leaderboards as engagement re-stimulants without diagnosing the loop's natural ceiling: extrinsic reward loops (badges → more completions → more badges) deplete over time as novelty fades and the catalog of earnable badges narrows for long-term users — the declining engagement from tenured users signals the reinforcing loop is hitting its balancing constraint, and adding more of the same extrinsic rewards extends the loop briefly without fixing the underlying motivation structure.
